Parting Stone, Inc.  ·  Confidential
Strategy Brief · Pre-Need Insurance Industry

How the Pre-Need
Industry Works — and
Where Parting Stone
Fits In

A plain-language guide to the players, money flows, and strategic opportunities for introducing Parting Stone into the pre-need funeral planning ecosystem.

MARCH 2026 · CONFIDENTIAL
A Note from the Founder
Justin Crowe
Founder & CEO · Parting Stone

Over the past year, I've been immersed in the pre-need funeral planning industry — beginning with Parting Stone's own direct-to-consumer pre-need offering, built and launched through a Pennsylvania merchandise trust. To date, we have sold approximately $85,000 in direct pre-need contracts. That work taught me how every player in this ecosystem is incentivized, what the structural challenges are, and where the real opportunity for Parting Stone lies.

The opportunity I'm presenting here is different from selling direct. It's about working inside the existing pre-need insurance infrastructure — through insurance companies, third-party marketing agencies, and the licensed pre-need sales agents who sit with families in funeral homes — to dramatically increase the volume of solidified remains contracts written across the country.

This approach solves a persistent problem in our business: motivating funeral directors to communicate with at-need families about solidified remains. Getting families to include solidified remains on their end-of-life plans before they die removes the decision from grieving families and removes the sales burden from funeral directors entirely. It also gives us guaranteed future revenue — a pipeline of contracted orders that increases the forward value of Parting Stone as a company.

The case for the insurance industry is equally compelling. Pre-need cremation policies are structurally low-value: insurers make money on the float — holding premium dollars in trust and earning a spread before payout. Cremation families generate far less policy value than burial families, and there are very few ways to meaningfully increase cremation contract size. Parting Stone at $2,495 MSRP can, in many cases, double the value of a cremation pre-need contract — solving a real revenue problem for insurers, agents, and funeral homes simultaneously.

Because the policy grows, every party benefits: the insurance company earns more on the larger float, the pre-need agent earns a larger commission, the funeral home earns a larger fee — and Parting Stone earns a guaranteed at-need wholesale order, invoiced at current pricing with no legacy pricing risk.

The pre-need space is opaque, moving fast, and full of strong personalities — from deeply traditional operators to aggressive newcomers like Precoa. This document is my attempt to map everything I've learned and lay out a clear path for how Parting Stone enters, competes, and profits. The enthusiasm we've already encountered at NGL — one of the two largest pre-need insurers in the country — suggests this isn't a niche ask; it's something the industry is ready for. What follows is a strategy brief, not a final plan. It is intended to inform a conversation with this board and our key stakeholders, whose alignment and approval will shape whatever we decide to do next.

Justin Crowe · Founder & CEO, Parting Stone, Inc.  ·  Santa Fe, New Mexico  ·  March 2026
01 · Overview

What Is Pre-Need Planning?

Pre-need planning is when a person arranges and pays for their own funeral before they die — typically funded through a life insurance policy written by a licensed pre-need insurer. The US industry processed over 517,000 contracts totaling $2.9 billion in face value in 2023 (LIMRA). The average buyer is 72–74, and plans are fulfilled approximately 14 years later at death. Every contract involves three separate agreements between the family, the insurance company, and the funeral home — and money flows in two distinct stages separated by over a decade.

👤
Player 1

The Family

Plans and pre-pays for future funeral services. Writes a check directly to the insurance company. Gets peace of mind that wishes are documented and fully funded — without burdening survivors.

🏦
Player 2

The Insurance Company

Issues the policy. Collects the premium, pays upfront commissions, and invests the rest in a conservative portfolio growing ~3–5%/yr. Guarantees the funeral home ~2%/yr and keeps the spread as margin. Reaches breakeven in 7–10 years.

🔗
Player 3 (optional)

Marketing Agency (TPM)

Designs the pre-need program for the insurer. Recruits, trains, and manages the licensed agents who sell plans at funeral homes. Takes the largest share of the commission pool. Precoa alone produces ~$400M of NGL's ~$750M annual volume.

🗣️
Player 4

Pre-Need Sales Agent

The licensed 1099 contractor who sits at the kitchen table with the family. This is the person who introduces Parting Stone. They work out of funeral homes, are trained and deployed by the marketing agency, and earn a direct commission cut on every plan they write. They are the most important relationship for Parting Stone's pre-need sales team to cultivate — agent belief and enthusiasm drives adoption.

⚱️
Player 5

The Funeral Home

Contracts with the family for goods and services. Hosts the pre-need sales agent. At time of death, receives the insurance payout from the carrier and uses it to fulfill the contracted services — paying suppliers (including Parting Stone) at current wholesale prices.

The Three-Contract Structure
📋
CONTRACT 1
Family ↔ Funeral Home

The goods & services agreement. Lists every item the family is pre-purchasing — cremation services, containers, merchandise. This is the document where Parting Stone appears as a line item.

📄
CONTRACT 2
Family ↔ Insurer

The insurance policy. The family writes their check to the insurance company. The policy value equals the total pre-need contract value including all merchandise add-ons.

🤝
CONTRACT 3
Funeral Home ↔ Insurer

The participation agreement. The funeral home agrees to accept the insurance payout as full payment for the contracted services at time of need.

Money Flow — Two Stages Separated by ~14 Years
Stage 1 · At Sale (Year 0)

The family pays. Commissions flow. Funds are invested.

The family writes a check to the insurance company for the full policy value. On a $7,500 plan (base cremation + Parting Stone add-on at $2,495):
Family → Insurance Company: $7,500
────────────────────
Commission pool (~20%): ~$1,500
· Marketing agency: ~8% (~$600)
· Writing agent: ~7% (~$525)
· Funeral home: ~5% (~$375)
────────────────────
Insurer invests: ~$6,000
Portfolio: 3–5%/yr growth
FH guaranteed: ~2%/yr
Insurer keeps the spread

The insurer's business model: invest premium for 7–10 years to break even, then earn profit on remaining duration. Thin margins, high volume.

Stage 2 · At Need (Year ~14)

The insured dies. Insurance company pays. Funeral home fulfills. Parting Stone produces solidified remains.

On the same $7,500 policy, after 14 years at the ~2% guaranteed rate:
$7,500 × (1.02)¹⁴ = $9,896
Insurance Co. pays $9,896 → Funeral Home
────────────────────
FH fulfills contracted services
FH pays Parting Stone wholesale
(current price at time of claim)
────────────────────
PS wholesale today: $1,195
PS wholesale yr 14 (~3%/yr): ~$1,808
FH margin on PS line: ~$1,491

Critical: Parting Stone invoices at current wholesale price at time of claim — not the price from when the plan was written. Zero pricing risk to Parting Stone.

Guaranteed Items

The funeral home commits to deliver these at the original pre-need price for the family — meaning the family will never be billed more regardless of inflation. The funeral home is protected from cost increases not by absorbing them, but because the insurance company guarantees an annual increase in the payout value (~2%/yr) over the life of the contract. That growing payout is what funds the funeral home's delivery of services at cost. Typical guaranteed items: own services, facilities, merchandise the funeral home controls.

Non-Guaranteed Items

Policy growth is applied, but if actual costs at time of need exceed the guaranteed payout, the difference can technically be billed to the family. Most funeral homes absorb minor gaps. Typical: third-party cash advances, cemetery fees, volatile-priced merchandise. Where the funeral home places Parting Stone (guaranteed vs. non-guaranteed) is a key decision they make at setup.

↑ Every pre-need plan involves these same mechanics regardless of which insurance carrier is used. Parting Stone fits as a merchandise line item on Contract 1 — the same way caskets and urns are funded today.

02 · Key Players & Market Size

Who's Who — Contracts Written & Revenue

There are two distinct types of organizations in this section and it's important not to conflate them. Insurance companies underwrite the policy, hold the premium, and are responsible for paying out at time of need. Third-Party Marketers (TPMs) are marketing agencies — they do not underwrite anything. They recruit, train, and deploy licensed sales agents at funeral homes on behalf of insurers, and take a share of the commission pool for managing that distribution. Parting Stone needs relationships with both, but the commercial terms and leverage dynamics are entirely different with each.

Insurance Company

Underwrites and issues the policy. Holds the premium. Guarantees payout at time of need. Bears actuarial risk. Examples: NGL, Homesteaders Life, FDLIC, Security National Life.

TPM — Third-Party Marketer

Pure marketing and distribution agency. No underwriting, no policy risk. Recruits agents, manages training, deploys at funeral homes. Takes the largest share of commissions for doing so. Examples: Precoa, Golden Considerations, Vantage Point, InVision Strategic Partners, Premier Preneed.

Industry total (LIMRA 2023): ~517,000 contracts · $2.9B face value · $5,609 average face across 16 participating companies. Company estimates below are derived from LIMRA market-share analysis, industry publications, and executive intelligence. Both contracts-per-year and face volume are shown for each.

Insurance Co. · Active Partnership Discussion · February 2026

National Guardian Life (NGL) ★ Contemplated Deal — Not Finalized

The second-largest pre-need insurer and Parting Stone's most advanced partnership conversation. NGL routes ~80% of its volume through Precoa (Precoa produces ~$400M of NGL's ~$750M annually). Zero direct funeral home relationships — 100% of sales flow through TPMs. Kenn Peterson is the key NGL contact and meetings have already taken place.

How the contemplated deal works: Parting Stone would appear as a merchandise line item on cremation pre-need contracts funded through NGL policies — the same way caskets, urns, and other merchandise are included today. A $2,495 Parting Stone add-on increases the total policy from ~$5,000 to ~$7,500. Agents introduce the choice naturally: "Which form of remains — cremated or solidified?" The larger policy generates more commission for every party (agency, agent, funeral home). At time of need, Parting Stone is paid its then-current wholesale price — no legacy pricing obligation, no pricing risk.

Parting Stone fee structure (TBD): The document from NGL discussions references an optional vendor fee of $50–100 per case at time of sale — sourced from the marketing agency's share — in addition to the at-need wholesale purchase at time of claim. This upfront fee structure is still subject to negotiation and reflects the Precoa-routed channel economics (not the direct insurer target of 25% of the commission pool).

$750Mannual premium volumeNGL figure
~70Kcremation policies/yr50% of total volume
~$400Mrouted through Precoa~53% of NGL volume
Kenn PetersonKey contact · meetings completedNGL relationship active
Insurance Co. · Recommended First

Homesteaders Life

Legacy company with deep direct funeral home relationships. Known for strong retention and willingness to offer upfront signing bonuses for meaningful new partnerships. Slow to move but well-capitalized. A priority direct-channel target.

120Kcontracts/yearindustry estimate
$673Mface value written/yr~23% market share
Insurance Co. · Direct Channel

Funeral Directors Life (FDLIC)

Historically direct-only with funeral homes — no TPM intermediary on their core book. Recently opened to Precoa for new growth, which has begun extracting margin. Brand identity is firmly "pro-funeral director." Best approached through their direct channel while it lasts.

~70Kcontracts/yearestimated, ~14% share
~$393Mface value written/yrLIMRA/industry est.
Insurance Co. · Recommended First · Most Creative

Security National Life (SNL)

Publicly traded, fast-growing, led by executives from outside the funeral industry — making them the most open to creative structures and outside-the-box proposals. Annual premium volume ~$100M, implying ~17,800 contracts at the industry average face. VP of Business Development: Sam Ashby (newly hired, receptive). President: Adam Quest. Parting Stone investor Jamie Meredith (C&J Financial) is an important player within SNL — a significant relationship advantage for Parting Stone going into this conversation.

TPM · Dominant Distributor

Precoa

Largest TPM by far — ~3× the next competitor. Manages commissions, trains agents, and deploys them at funeral homes nationwide. Estimated ~$700M managed volume, implying ~125K contracts equivalent.

~125Kcontracts managed/yrderived from $700M ÷ avg face
$700Mmanaged volume/yrindustry estimate
Third-Party Marketers (TPMs)
— marketing agencies only, no policy underwriting

TPMs are pure distribution organizations. They recruit and manage the licensed agents who sell pre-need plans at funeral homes on behalf of insurers. They take the largest share of the commission pool — often 35%+ — for owning that distribution relationship. Parting Stone's relationship with TPMs is primarily about agent access and training; the commercial terms are subordinate to insurer agreements. Key intelligence: Precoa is the dominant player, but several others operate in the market with less aggressive terms.

DOMINANT · ~3× next competitor

Precoa

The largest TPM by a significant margin. Manages agents for NGL (~$400M of NGL's ~$750M) and FDLIC. Known for aggressive exclusivity demands and hardball negotiating tactics. High volume, but comes with significant strategic risk.

~$700M managed · ~125K contracts/yr
MID-TIER TPM · Investor Relationship

Golden Considerations

Mid-size TPM operating primarily in the Southeast and Midwest. Less aggressive than Precoa on exclusivity terms. May represent an earlier, lower-risk entry point for agent relationships.

Volume: industry intelligence only
MID-TIER TPM

Vantage Point

Regional TPM with established funeral home agent networks. Operates with less market dominance than Precoa, potentially offering more favorable commission splits and contract terms for a new merchandise partner.

Volume: industry intelligence only
MID-TIER TPM

InVision Strategic Partners

Growing TPM with a strategy-forward positioning. Less volume than Precoa but potentially more collaborative on program design. Worth early conversations as a source of market intelligence and potential pilot relationships.

Volume: industry intelligence only
MID-TIER TPM

Premier Preneed

Established TPM with regional funeral home agent relationships. Part of the broader competitive landscape that Precoa must contend with. May provide an alternative distribution channel if Precoa terms prove untenable.

Volume: industry intelligence only
Strategic note: Detailed financials are not publicly available for mid-tier TPMs. The volume figures above are based on industry intelligence from the advisor conversation. The primary value of these relationships is agent access — Parting Stone's pre-need sales team would work with TPM-managed agents the same way it works with direct-insurer agents. Precoa's dominance does not make the others irrelevant; even a small TPM relationship established early provides negotiating context and potential leverage.
Source note: NGL (140,000 contracts/yr) and Homesteaders (120,000 contracts/yr) figures based on industry estimates and LIMRA market-share analysis. SNL's ~$100M premium figure implies ~17,800 contracts using the LIMRA 2023 industry average face of $5,609. FDLIC contracts estimated from LIMRA market share analysis. Precoa is a TPM (not an insurer) — the $700M figure represents policies it manages on behalf of insurers (primarily NGL and FDLIC). Industry totals: LIMRA/LIC 2023 Annual Preneed Survey, 16 participants, 517,000 contracts, $2.9B face value.
03 · Age Distribution & Commission Model

Who's Buying Pre-Need — and What That Means for Parting Stone's Upfront Revenue

Commission rates in pre-need insurance are not flat — they are a function of the insured's age at the time of purchase, because the insurer's risk (and thus the available premium spread) is directly tied to how long until the policy is likely to pay out. The younger the buyer, the higher the commission available. Parting Stone's upfront revenue per contract varies materially depending on the age distribution of buyers.

Pre-Need Buyer Age Distribution

Based on LIMRA/LIC industry data: average buyer age 73.6 (2020 survey). Distribution calibrated to match this mean and the industry's observed concentration in the 70–84 band. Peak buying cohort is 75–79.

Age Band % of Buyers Share Comm. Rate PS Upfront*

~3%$17
Weighted average buyer age: 73.6 ~11.8% $58.82

How the Commission Model Works

The total policy face value (e.g. $5,609 industry average) generates a commission pool. But Parting Stone is a line item within that policy — the solidified remains service at $2,495 MSRP. Commission on the Parting Stone portion is calculated only on Parting Stone's line item, not on the full policy value. The solidified remains line is separated out, and Parting Stone's commission share is calculated exclusively on that amount.

FULL POLICY FACE: ~$5,609 avg
(base cremation services)
+ PS LINE ITEM: $2,495 MSRP
─────────────────────────
PS COMMISSION POOL:
$2,495 × COMM. RATE (age-based)
= TOTAL PS POOL
× PARTING STONE'S SHARE (~25%)
= PS UPFRONT REVENUE
─────────────────────────
EXAMPLE: Age 65, $2,495 PS face
$2,495 × 16% = $399 PS pool
$399 × 25% = ~$100 PS upfront

*PS upfront column in the table to the left uses $2,495 MSRP as the policy face for Parting Stone's line item specifically. PS never shares in commissions on the broader policy — only on the solidified remains service line. Commission rates sourced from industry practitioner schedules (blended = 57% single-pay + 40% multi-pay per LIMRA 2023 mix).

Why Rates Compress With Age

Commission rates decline as the insured ages because the insurer has less time to earn an investment return before a claim is made. A 50-year-old policy may sit invested for 35+ years. An 85-year-old policy may pay out in 3–5 years — so the insurer can offer far less commission from that shorter investment window.

Ages Under 65
~17–19%
Blended rate. Long investment runway. PS upfront: $80–96/contract.
Ages 65–79 · Peak
~11–16%
Most buyers fall here. PS upfront: $57–80/contract. Weighted avg: $74.
Ages 80+
~3–7%
Short window for insurer. PS upfront: $17–36/contract. At-need value still intact.

Rates sourced from industry practitioner schedules (blended = 57% single-pay + 40% multi-pay + 3% annuity per LIMRA 2023 mix). PS upfront column uses $2,495 MSRP as add-on policy face.

Distribution of the Commission Pool on a Parting Stone Add-On

On the $2,495 Parting Stone add-on at the weighted-average commission rate (~11.8%), the total commission pool is ~$294. Here is how that splits — and what Parting Stone's share looks like depending on the channel:

Party
Share of ~$294 commission pool (11.8% of $2,495 MSRP — blended age-weighted)
Insurance Company
~45% · $132 margin/overhead
TPM (Precoa)
~35% · $103 distribution fee
Sales Agent (1099)
~12% · $35
Parting Stone ✦
~5% · ~$15 via Precoa
Funeral Home
~3% · $9
Parting Stone Commission — Three Scenarios
Via Precoa
~$15
~5% of pool. Precoa takes the majority of the distribution margin. High volume, but pre-need team cannot be self-funded from commissions alone.
Other TPMs
~$44
~15% of pool. Smaller third-party marketers are less dominant and leave more room for Parting Stone's share in the distribution agreement.
Direct Insurer ★ Target
~$74
~25% of pool. Insurers that work directly with funeral homes — no TPM intermediary. Parting Stone is targeting 25% commission in direct insurer agreements with carriers like SNL and Homesteaders.

Two Revenue Streams

Unlike typical insurance add-ons, Parting Stone plans have a near-100% eventual utilization rate — every pre-arranged family intends to use it. The at-need sale is not speculative; it is deferred.

MSRP: $2,495
─────────────────
Stream 1 — Upfront:
~$59–$74/contract
(age-weighted avg,
direct insurer target)
Recognized immediately

Stream 2 — At-Need:
$1,195 wholesale
Recognized at death
─────────────────
Range: $17 (age 90)
to $96 (under 50)
via direct insurer
04 · 10-Year Revenue Forecast

Actuarial-Based Revenue Model: 2026–2035

Revenue is modeled using SSA 2021 Period Life Table mortality data for an average pre-need purchaser aged 73.6. Both wholesale price and MSRP are assumed to grow at 3%/yr — matching the guaranteed annual payout increase NGL provides funeral homes. Parting Stone invoices at current wholesale at time of claim, so price appreciation accrues entirely to PS. The model is built partner-by-partner, reflecting a realistic ramp from direct insurers in Year 1 through full channel deployment by Year 5.

~360
new contracts/yr
SNL only

new contracts/yr

new contracts/yr
+ NGL/Precoa + FDLIC + 2 small TPMs
~300K addressable. NGL deal closes — blended commission (~$40/contract through Precoa channel). FDLIC direct adds volume. Big volume inflection.

~5,000
new contracts/yr
+ Additional mid-size insurer
All Year 3 partners maturing. Additional insurer relationship activated. Pre-need team fully operational across all channels.

Revenue Timing Insight

The business compounds faster than it looks. Because the average buyer is 73.6, contracts begin paying out within 3–5 years of signing. By Year 10, nearly half of all contracts signed in 2026 will have generated their $1,195+ wholesale order.

Revenue in Years 1–2 is primarily upfront commissions. The at-need stream accelerates sharply from Year 5 onward as the 2026–2028 cohorts enter peak mortality bands (age 78–85). By Year 10, at-need revenue is running at 3–4× the annual upfront commission stream.

Key implication: Every contract signed in 2026–2027 is worth substantially more than its $74 upfront commission suggests — it carries a near-certain at-need order of $1,400–1,500+ (3% price growth over 7–10 years) behind it.

Model Assumptions

Addressable Market by Partner NGL: 140,000 contracts/yr · Homesteaders: 120,000/yr · FDLIC: ~70,000/yr · SNL: ~18,000/yr. Total addressable at full deployment: ~348K–500K contracts/yr. Penetration % = share of each insurer's annual volume that includes a Parting Stone add-on. Partners added sequentially per ramp above.
Commission Model & Price Growth Direct insurers (SNL, Homesteaders, FDLIC): target 25% of commission pool = ~$74/contract upfront. NGL/Precoa channel: ~$40/contract blended. Both MSRP and wholesale grow 3%/yr. By Year 10, at-need wholesale = ~$1,605. PS invoices at current wholesale price at time of claim — zero pricing risk.
Buyer Age & Mortality Industry average purchaser age: 73.6 (LIMRA/LIC 2020 survey). Mortality from SSA 2021 Period Life Table (blended M/F). ~9% of contracts fulfill within 3 yrs · ~18% within 5 yrs · ~46% within 10 yrs · ~70% within 15 yrs. Near-zero lapse rate — unlike traditional insurance, families do not cancel pre-need plans.

New pre-need contracts written per year by insurer, penetration rate (% of each insurer's annual volume), and projected revenue. Contract count ramps linearly from each insurer's entry year to peak penetration over 4 years, then holds flat. Revenue grows beyond plateau solely due to 3%/yr price escalation on both commission rates and wholesale price — not additional contracts. Penetration rates represent Parting Stone's share of each insurer's annual cremation pre-need contract volume.

Peak penetration: All four insurers: 3.0% · 10,440 total contracts/yr

05 · Organizational Requirement

A New Branch of Parting Stone — The Pre-Need Sales Team

Entering the pre-need market is not simply a distribution agreement — it requires building a dedicated internal team. Just as Parting Stone has a B2B at-need sales team that supports funeral directors who sell to families at the time of death, the pre-need channel requires a parallel team whose job is to support and train the pre-need agents who are selling to consumers in advance. Without this team, even a signed insurer agreement produces little volume.

Existing Team

B2B At-Need Sales Team

Works directly with funeral home owners and funeral directors to establish Parting Stone as an at-need offering. Supports fulfillment, trains staff on the product, and maintains funeral home relationships. Revenue recognized at time of death when the funeral home places an order.

Customer: Funeral home / funeral director
Revenue trigger: Family places at-need order
New Team Required

Pre-Need Sales Team

Works with pre-need insurance agents (1099 contractors at funeral homes) to train them on presenting Parting Stone as part of a pre-need plan. Attends pre-need sales training events, maintains insurer relationships, and drives plan adoption across a distributed network of agents who each sell to individual consumers. Revenue recognized in two waves: upfront commission on signing, then wholesale at death.

Customer: Pre-need insurance agent / insurer
Revenue trigger: Contract signed (upfront) + death (at-need)

Why Upfront Commission Is the Key to Building This Team

The central operational challenge in entering the pre-need market is funding: the most valuable revenue stream — $1,195 wholesale at-need per contract — is deferred by an average of 7–12 years. A new pre-need sales team cannot be sustained on future revenue alone. Upfront commission income is what makes the pre-need sales team self-financing.

This creates a direct link between the commission structure Parting Stone negotiates with insurers and the pace at which the pre-need division can be built out. A deal that yields ~$59/contract upfront (via a direct insurer) can fund team salaries, training events, and partner development from Year 1. A deal that yields ~$15/contract (via Precoa) requires substantially more volume before the team is self-sustaining — or requires a signing bonus to bridge the gap.

The pre-need sales team should be thought of as a capital allocation decision: the size, speed, and sustainability of the team is a direct function of the upfront commission rate secured in partnership agreements. Securing favorable upfront terms is not just a financial preference — it is the prerequisite for building the team at all.

Upfront Commission Scenarios
~$59
per contract via direct insurer (20% of commission pool). At 1,000 contracts/yr: ~$59K upfront annually — enough to seed 1 dedicated hire.
~$15
per contract via Precoa (5% of commission pool). At 1,000 contracts/yr: ~$15K upfront annually — not enough to sustain a team without a separate signing bonus.
Signing Bonus
Signing bonuses are a standard industry practice — insurance companies routinely pay funeral homes a one-time bonus when they commit to a new pre-need program, reflecting the long-term revenue value of that relationship. Because Parting Stone represents a potentially significant new revenue stream for any insurer (every PS contract increases the total policy face value), there is a strong case that one or more insurers — particularly Homesteaders, which is known for this practice — would be willing to offer Parting Stone a signing bonus as well. A negotiated signing bonus could directly fund the pre-need team from day one, removing the dependency on commission income in the early months and allowing the program to launch at full speed.
06 · Go-to-Market Strategy

Three Paths to Market: What Are We Optimizing For?

The fundamental strategic question is not simply which partner to sign with — it is what you are optimizing for in Year 1–3: maximizing upfront recognized commission revenue (which funds the pre-need team), maximizing the backlog of future at-need orders (which drives long-term revenue), or throwing everything at the wall simultaneously to build the largest possible pre-need contract base as fast as possible. Each path has a distinct financial profile and organizational implication.

Path A · Optimize Upfront Commission

Direct Insurers Only
SNL · Homesteaders · FDLIC

The trade-off: Maximize upfront recognized commission (~$59/contract) to fund the pre-need sales team — but sacrifice short-term volume. The at-need backlog builds slowly while direct channels are established.

  • ~$59 upfront per contract — 4× more than through Precoa
  • Direct insurer relationships with better long-term margin control
  • Signing bonuses available (especially Homesteaders) to fund team launch
  • Sam Ashby (SNL) and Jamie Meredith are warm contacts available now
  • Learn market pricing before entering Precoa negotiations
  • SNL's ~18K contracts/yr is a fraction of Precoa-managed volume
  • Homesteaders moves slowly — 12–24 months to meaningful volume
  • Pre-need backlog builds gradually; at-need revenue lags by years
Path B · Optimize At-Need Backlog

NGL + Precoa First
Maximum Volume

The trade-off: Sacrifice upfront commission (~$15/contract) in exchange for rapid pre-need contract volume through Precoa's massive distribution. Builds a large at-need backlog faster, but the pre-need team cannot be funded from commissions alone — requiring a signing bonus or separate capital.

  • Access to ~125K Precoa-managed contracts/yr — 7× the SNL volume
  • NGL relationship already warm via Kenn Peterson
  • Fastest path to building a large at-need order backlog
  • Precoa handles agent training and distribution logistics
  • Only ~$15 upfront per contract — pre-need team cannot be self-funded from commissions
  • Precoa has a notoriously aggressive, SCI-like negotiating posture (see Risks section)
  • Exclusivity demands will likely restrict future direct insurer relationships
  • Parting Stone loses direct relationship with funeral homes
Path C · All Channels Simultaneously

Pursue All Partners at Once
Maximum Backlog, Lower Commission

The trade-off: Approach NGL/Precoa and all direct insurers simultaneously to maximize the total number of pre-need contracts written as fast as possible. Upfront commissions will not be optimized — Precoa volume will dilute the blended rate. But this path builds the largest possible at-need order backlog in the shortest time frame.

  • ↑Fastest possible growth in the pre-need contract backlog
  • ↑At-need revenue curve begins inflecting in Years 5–7 across all channels
  • ↑Establishes market presence and pre-need brand awareness broadly
  • !Blended upfront commission is lower — pre-need team requires outside funding or signing bonus to sustain
  • !Organizational complexity: managing multiple insurer and TPM relationships simultaneously requires significant internal bandwidth
  • !Risk of Precoa inserting exclusivity provisions that retroactively foreclose direct channels
Recommended starting point: Pursue Path A first to establish favorable commission terms, fund the pre-need team from upfront income, and learn market pricing before engaging Precoa. Once direct insurer agreements are signed with explicit carve-outs protecting the direct channel, introduce Precoa/NGL negotiations from a position of knowledge and leverage. The path C "all at once" approach is viable if Parting Stone can secure a signing bonus large enough to fund the pre-need team independent of commission income — making it a capital allocation decision rather than a commission optimization question.
07 · Risk Assessment

Key Risks to Manage

Each major risk identified through the industry conversation, rated by likelihood and potential impact to Parting Stone's pre-need strategy.

HIGH RISK

Precoa's Aggressive Negotiating Posture

Precoa has a well-documented reputation for aggressive, SCI-like deal tactics: brutal exclusivity demands, deliberate negotiating slowdowns designed to exhaust the other side into unfavorable terms, and retroactive margin capture on existing funeral home relationships. This is not speculation — it is their standard operating model. NGL, which is the partner Parting Stone has made the most progress with, routes the vast majority of its business through Precoa. Any NGL agreement that flows through Precoa should be approached with this in mind: entering without leverage or alternatives signed is entering from a position of weakness.

Mitigation — Short-Term Contract Strategy: One viable approach is to pursue a Precoa deal with open eyes — accepting that the initial terms may not be ideal — but insisting on a 2–3 year contract term rather than a long-term lock-in. A short term limits downside exposure and creates an early renegotiation window. By Year 2–3, Parting Stone will have real volume data, direct insurer alternatives in place, and meaningful leverage to negotiate substantially better terms. A short first deal with Precoa is not a concession — it is a deliberate strategic position.
HIGH RISK

Pre-Need Team Funding Gap

At-need wholesale revenue ($1,195) won't arrive in meaningful volume until 5–10 years after contracts are signed. Without sufficient upfront commission income (~$59/contract via direct insurer) or a one-time signing bonus, Parting Stone cannot sustain a dedicated pre-need sales team during the critical early growth phase. The commission structure negotiated in initial agreements directly determines how quickly this team can be built and how long it takes to become self-funding.

MEDIUM RISK

Precoa Exclusivity Blocking Direct Channels

If Parting Stone enters a Precoa deal before signing direct insurer agreements, Precoa will likely attempt to insert language requiring all funeral home pre-need sales to flow through them — effectively locking out the higher-commission direct insurer channel. This is a foreseeable negotiating tactic, not a surprise. Parting Stone should expect this language to appear and treat it as a hard red line: any exclusivity provision that prevents working with direct insurers (SNL, Homesteaders, FDLIC) simultaneously is a deal-breaker, not a negotiating chip. The sequencing of deal-making is the primary defense: direct insurer agreements with explicit multi-channel carve-outs should be signed before any Precoa/NGL negotiation begins.

Expected language to watch for: "All pre-need merchandise sales at participating funeral homes must be processed through [Precoa/TPM name]." This clause, if accepted, would retroactively capture direct insurer relationships. It should be identified early in any draft agreement and removed or carved out explicitly before signing.
MEDIUM RISK

Sales Agent Adoption

Even with a distribution partner signed, pre-need agents must believe in Parting Stone to include it in their pitch. Industry data indicates that roughly 20% of agents generate 80% of sales — the long tail is hard to activate without ongoing investment in training, materials, and agent incentives. This is one of the core responsibilities of the pre-need sales team.

LOW RISK

Competitor Replication

No known direct competitors in solidified remains pre-need. Funeral homes occasionally attempt in-house programs but abandon them once operational complexity becomes clear — consistent with Parting Stone's active patent protection strategy against Sacred Stones and Remember Me Pebbles.

LOW RISK

Insurer Relationship Concentration

If Parting Stone builds its pre-need channel predominantly through one insurer (e.g., NGL via Precoa), that insurer's priorities, policy changes, or contract renegotiations could significantly impact Parting Stone's access to agents. Maintaining parallel relationships with multiple insurers — even at smaller volumes — is a hedge against concentration risk.

08 · Proposed Roadmap

Proposed Action Sequence: 2026–2030

Important context: Parting Stone has already met with NGL and NGL has expressed interest — meaning this process is already in motion and does not follow a clean sequential order. The roadmap below reflects that reality: NGL is a live conversation happening now, and the goal is to move quickly across all direct-insurer relationships simultaneously to build leverage before any deal is signed.

⚑
This is a proposed roadmap, not an approved plan. The action sequence, partner priorities, timing, and resource commitments outlined below require the review, alignment, and formal approval of the Parting Stone board of advisors and relevant key stakeholders before any binding steps are taken. This document is a strategy brief intended to inform that conversation.
Phase 1 · NOW — Q2 2026 · ACTIVE

Build the Proposal & Align Internally

Draft a formal pre-need partnership proposal and have Parting Stone's key players conduct a thorough red-flag review. This proposal will serve as the foundation for all insurer conversations. Simultaneously, engage Jamie Meredith (C&J Financial) — a Parting Stone investor and key player within SNL — to advance the SNL relationship and secure warm introductions to other target insurers. Kenn Peterson remains the active contact for NGL. The genuine enthusiasm Kenn has shown is an early signal that receptivity to this kind of merchandise partnership exists at the highest levels of the industry — which is precisely why moving on direct insurer relationships simultaneously, rather than sequentially, is urgent.

Phase 2 · Q2–Q3 2026 · URGENT

Active Meetings — Homesteaders, SNL & Continued NGL Dialogue

Begin direct meetings with Homesteaders and SNL while continuing the active NGL conversation. The goal of this phase is to develop a clear picture of market appetite: what commission splits are realistic, whether signing bonuses are on the table, and what contract language each insurer will and won't accept. Because NGL has already expressed interest, these parallel meetings are not just prospecting — they are intelligence-gathering and leverage-building for the NGL deal. Understanding what Homesteaders and SNL will offer gives Parting Stone a floor and a BATNA before committing to any NGL/Precoa terms.

Phase 3 · Q3–Q4 2026

Sign Pilot Agreements — Direct Insurers First

Negotiate and sign pilot agreements with whichever direct insurer(s) offer the most favorable terms — targeting 20–25% of the commission pool (~$59–$74/contract at the blended age average), a signing bonus to fund the pre-need sales team, and explicit contract carve-outs protecting Parting Stone's right to work with additional insurers. Signed agreements immediately become leverage in any subsequent NGL/Precoa discussion. The pre-need sales team launch begins here: the first hire's job is to train agents at pilot insurer funeral homes on presenting Parting Stone as part of their pre-need plan.

Phase 4 · 2027–2028

Formalize NGL Agreement — Navigate Precoa With Leverage

With signed direct-insurer pilots, real volume data, and an operational pre-need sales team, return to NGL for a formal agreement. Given that NGL routes ~80% of volume through Precoa, any NGL deal will require navigating Precoa's notoriously aggressive negotiating posture. Arrive at this conversation with: (1) competing signed agreements as alternatives, (2) Kenn Peterson as the NGL relationship anchor, and (3) specific red lines on exclusivity — no retroactive capture of direct insurer channels already under contract, no prohibition on maintaining existing funeral home relationships. If Precoa terms cannot be made acceptable in Year 1, consider a deliberate short-term deal (2–3 years) at less-than-ideal terms as a traction play — accepting the initial terms in exchange for a renegotiation window before Year 3, by which time Parting Stone will have leverage, data, and alternatives.

Phase 5 · 2028–2030

Scale the Pre-Need Division

With multiple insurer agreements active and commission income funding a growing pre-need team, formalize the pre-need division as a distinct P&L within Parting Stone. The at-need revenue curve inflects sharply around Year 5 as contracts signed in 2026–2027 enter peak mortality bands. By this point, the channel should be producing material annual at-need wholesale revenue while the pre-need team continues to grow the forward order book across all insurer relationships.

Section 09

Key Relationships

Parting Stone's entry into the pre-need insurance market is relationship-driven. The contacts below represent our current network across insurers, third-party marketers, and strategic advisors. Warmth and status reflect current standing as of Q1 2026.

Kenn Peterson
NGL · Key Contact
National Guardian Life (NGL)
Key NGL contact. One meeting held in Naples — actively working on next steps. NGL writes ~140K pre-need contracts/yr and represents our largest addressable insurer relationship.
Connection: Tom Anderson introduction
Active — next steps in progress
Jamie Meredith
SNL · PS Investor
CEO, C&J Financial
Parting Stone investor and CEO of C&J Financial. Important player within Security National Life — our most natural entry point to SNL. Warm, active relationship.
Connection: Parting Stone investor
Warm — advance SNL relationship
Josh Rae
TPM · Investor Connection
Golden Considerations (TPM)
Son of original Parting Stone investor Bob Rae (deceased). Now active in the family business — Golden Considerations, a third-party marketer (TPM). Relationship is not close and he may have a competing pre-need interest, but reachable for an introductory conversation.
Connection: Son of PS investor Bob Rae
Light — can open a conversation
Sam Ashby
SNL · Acquaintance
Security National Life (SNL)
Industry contact Justin has known for years. Good relationship at a personal level, though more of an acquaintance than a strategic partner. SNL VP of Business Development.
Connection: Long-standing industry contact
Acquaintance — warm intro possible
Tom Anderson
Connector · PS Investor
Industry Veteran · PS Investor
Parting Stone investor and seasoned industry contact. Directly connected Justin with Kenn Peterson at NGL — making him the key bridge to our most important insurer relationship.
